blurb: Verse collections that are similar to the Dhammapada of the Pali Khuddhaka Nikāya.

children.blurb: The Pali version of this famous text, consisting of 423 verses organized into memorable themes. It is the most widely read of the early texts, and has been translated many times into many languages. Versions of this text are found in Chinese, Tibetan, and several Indic languages, attesting to its timeless, universal appeal.

The Udānavarga or “Collection of Inspired Sayings” is a Sarvāstivādin Dhammapada-style text. At 1044 verses, it is over twice as long as the Pali Dhammapada, and the longest of all the Dhammapadas. The manuscript was among the Sanskrit texts discovered at Turfan. The SuttaCentral text is sourced from the Titus edition prepared by Gippert and Kupfer, and based on the edition by Bernhard (1965). There are Chinese and Tibetan translations of this text.
A critical edition by H. Nakatani of the Sanskrit manuscript on wood from Subaši, Bibliothèque nationale de Paris, Pelliot Collection, 1987–1988.

The Patna Dharmapada is based on a manuscript discovered in Tibet by Rāhula Saṅkṛtyāyana in 1934, written in proto-Bengali script, and dated around the twelfth century. It is in a distinct variety of Sanskritized Prākrit, and has been identified by Skilling and Mizuno as belonging to the Sāmmatīya school. The SuttaCentral text is based on the edition by Margaret Cone (1989), digitized by Ānandajoti.

The Gāndhārī Dharmapada (also known as the Khotan Dhammapada) is from a birch-bark manuscript in Kharoṣṭhī script discovered at Kohmāri Mazār near Khotan in Xinjiang in 1892. It dates to the early centuries CE, and is one of the oldest surviving Buddhist manuscripts. It was edited and published by Brough (1962), and was digitized by Gandhari.org.
Fragments of a Dhammapada in Gāndhārī
Fragments of another Dhammapada in Gāndhārī

Two fascicles compiled by Dharmatrāta 法救, T 210.4.559–574. It was brought by the Indian monk Vighna 維祇難 to Wuchang 武昌, China in 224 C.E., and translated by Zhu Jiangyan 竺將炎 and Zhi Qian 支謙. It is a collection of verses comprising the basic teachings of Buddhism which is similar to the Pali Dhammapada. The Chinese version contains 758 verses. There are four Chinese translations, dated CE 224, 290–306, 399, and 980–1001.

children.translated_name: Sayings of the Dharma With Stories (Chinese)
children.blurb: Four fascicles, compiled by Dharmatrāta and translated by Faju 法炬, and Fali 法立 during the years of Huidi 惠帝 (290–306 C.E.) in the Western Jin dynasty 西晉 at Luoyang. Also known by the titles of 法句本末經, 法句喩經, 法喩經, and 法句譬經. Written in thirty-nine chapters, it is, like the 法句經 (T 210), closely related to the Dhammapada of the Pāli Canon, and about two thirds of this text are the same as T 210. It is composed of explanatory text added to the verses of the Dharmapāda.

The Indian original text compiled by Dharmatrāta 法救 consisted of 33 chapters and about 1000 verses, and it was translated by Saṁghabhadra 僧伽跋澄 and Zhu Fonian 竺佛念 during 398–399. The present T 212 has 34 chapters, and its chapters correspond to the present Udānavarga, which has 33 chapters and 1050 verses, except that it has two separated chapters, i.e. the chapter of non-carelessness 無放逸品 and the chapter of carelessness 放逸品, for the chapter 'Apramādavarga' of the Udānavarga. Like T 211, this text also provides the verses along with the background stories in prose. When the verses are same with those of T 210, the translators of T 211 followed the translation of T 210 rather than did a new translation.

Also attributed to Dharmatrāta’s compilation, this was translated by Tianxizai 天息災 (?–1000), who arrived at the capital of Song dynasty in 980. It has 33 chapters corresponding to the chapters of the Udānavarga, but, unlike T 212, this text has only verses without commentaries. The translator of T 213 followed the translation of T 212, and he also followed the translation of T 210 when those verses are not in T 212 but same with T 210.
A Tibetan translation of the Udānavarga, attributed to Dharmatrāta. There is a commentary on this text by Prajñāvarman.
